metadata.notes: Content overhaul to Wingspan standard — Rogue B, March 2026. Sources: official Stonemaier rulebook (Essential Edition), BGG FAQ, r/boardgames strategy threads.

key_rulings.text: Your personal score is the LOWER of your two city scores. The player with the highest personal score wins.
Both partners must agree on where to place tiles in their shared city. If partners cannot agree, the player whose turn it is to break ties decides.
Shops score based on straight lines (same row or column): 1 shop = 2 pts, 2 = 5 pts, 3 = 10 pts, 4 = 15 pts, 5 = 20 pts.
Factories score based on ranking across ALL cities: most factories = 4 pts each, second-most = 3 pts each, third-most = 2 pts each, all others = 1 pt each.
Taverns score by number of different subtypes: 1 type = 1 pt, 2 types = 4 pts, 3 types = 9 pts, 4 types (complete set) = 17 pts.
Each house scores points equal to the number of OTHER unique tile types present in the city, not counting houses themselves.
Duplex tiles must be placed as a 1x2 domino (horizontal or vertical). Both halves go into the same city and cannot be split.
In Round 1, pass remaining tiles to the LEFT after each selection.
In Round 3, pass remaining tiles to the RIGHT after each selection.
If tied on personal score, the player whose higher-scoring city has more points wins.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Tiles
You'll find two types of building tiles in the box:
- **108 single building tiles** — These are square tiles in 6 types (18 of each). Each type has its own color and scoring rule:
  - **Shops** (blue) — Retail storefronts. They score by forming **straight lines** in the same row or column.
  - **Factories** (gray) — Industrial buildings. They score by **competitive ranking** — the city with the most factories across the whole game scores the most per factory.
  - **Parks** (green) — Natural green spaces. They score by **connected groups** (tiles sharing a side).
  - **Taverns** (red) — Food and drink spots. They come in 4 subtypes (music, drink, food, bed) and score by **variety** — collecting different subtypes.
  - **Offices** (purple) — Professional buildings. They score by **cumulative count** in the city, with a bonus for being adjacent to taverns.
  - **Houses** (brown/yellow) — Residential dwellings. They score by **diversity** — each house is worth points equal to the number of other unique tile types in the city.
- **24 duplex tiles** — These are larger tiles showing 2 building types side by side. Each one takes up 2 squares in your city grid. You'll only use these in Round 2.

#### Markers & Reference
- **7 city tokens** — These go between neighboring players to mark each shared city.
- **1 scoreboard** — For tracking final city scores (0-80 range).
- **7 scoring markers** — One per player, placed on the scoreboard at the end.
- **7 player reference cards** — Double-sided cards showing all 6 tile scoring rules. Grab one and keep it in front of you — you'll check it constantly.
- **1 seating randomizer card**

#### Automa (Solo/2-Player)
- **Automa tiles and reference card** — These are for solo and 2-player games only. The Automa simulates extra players so the drafting and dual-city mechanics still work properly.

#### Other
- **1 rulebook** — Covers standard rules, solo rules, and Capitals expansion content (Essential Edition).

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Setup
subtopics.content:
#### How the Game Works
Between Two Cities is a **semi-cooperative** tile-drafting game. Here's the twist: you're building **two cities at once** — one shared with the neighbor on your left, one shared with the neighbor on your right. Each city is a **4x4 grid** of building tiles (16 squares total). The game plays over 3 rounds, and at the end, your personal score is the **lower** of your two cities' scores. (So you can't just pour everything into one city and ignore the other.)

#### Setting Up
1. **Sit everyone in a circle.** Use the seating randomizer card to determine order.
2. **Place a city token** between each pair of neighbors. That token marks your shared city — you'll build the grid right there on the table.
3. **Separate the tiles:** Put all 108 single building tiles in a face-down supply pile. Put all 24 duplex tiles in a separate face-down pile.
4. **Hand each player** a scoring marker and a reference card.
5. **Place the scoreboard** where everyone can reach it.

#### City Grid Layout
Each city is built as a **4x4 grid** — that's 4 rows by 4 columns, 16 total squares. Single tiles fill 1 square. Duplex tiles fill 2 adjacent squares (placed as a 1x2 domino, either horizontally or vertically). Once you place a tile, it **cannot be moved** — so think carefully before committing.

By the end of the game, each city will be completely filled: single tiles from Rounds 1 and 3, plus duplex tiles from Round 2, covering all 16 squares.

#### Checkpoint
Each player should have: a reference card and a scoring marker. Between every pair of neighbors, there should be a city token. On the table: single tiles in one face-down supply, duplex tiles in another, and the scoreboard visible to all.

subtopics.title: Round-by-Round Flow
subtopics.content:
#### Round 1 — Single Tiles (Draft Left)
Here's how your first round plays out, step by step:
1. Each player draws **7 single tiles** from the supply. Look at them, but keep them hidden from other players.
2. **Pick 2 tiles** from your hand — everyone chooses at the same time.
3. **Reveal** your 2 chosen tiles face-up. Now place **1 tile** in your left city and **1 tile** in your right city. Talk to your city partners about where each tile should go — you both need to **agree** on placement.
4. **Pass your remaining tiles** (5 of them) to the player on your **left**.
5. Repeat: pick 2 from the new hand you receive, reveal, place, pass left.
6. Keep going until tiles run out. On your final pick, you'll have 3 tiles — choose 2, and **discard the last 1 face-down**.

After Round 1, each city has **5 single tiles** placed in its grid.

#### Round 2 — Duplex Tiles
This round works differently — no drafting, just choosing:
1. Each player draws **3 duplex tiles** from the duplex supply.
2. **Keep 2** of them. **Discard 1** face-down.
3. **Reveal** your 2 chosen duplex tiles. Place **1 duplex** in your left city and **1 duplex** in your right city. Remember: each duplex occupies 2 squares and must be placed as a horizontal or vertical domino — you can't split the halves between cities.

After Round 2, each city has 5 single tiles + 2 duplex tiles (covering 4 squares) = **9 squares filled**.

#### Round 3 — Single Tiles (Draft Right)
Same process as Round 1, with one key difference:
1. Each player draws **7 single tiles** from the supply.
2. Draft exactly like Round 1 — pick 2, reveal, place, pass — but this time pass remaining tiles to the **right** instead of the left.
3. On your final pick, discard 1 tile face-down as before.

After Round 3, every city is fully built — all **16 squares** of the 4x4 grid are filled.

#### Partnership Communication
This is important: you share each city with a partner, and you need to **talk openly** about what the city needs. Discuss scoring opportunities, what types are missing, where tiles should go. Good communication isn't optional — it's a core part of the game.

(If you and your partner genuinely can't agree on placement, the player whose turn it is to break ties decides. Tie-breaking privilege alternates each round.)

subtopics.title: Tile Scoring Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Shops (Blue) — Straight Line Scoring
Shops are retail storefronts that score based on **straight lines** — shops in the same row or same column (not diagonal). The longer your line, the better:
- 1 shop: **2 points**
- 2 shops in a line: **5 points**
- 3 shops in a line: **10 points**
- 4 shops in a line: **15 points**
- 5 shops in a line: **20 points** (possible with landscape mats)

If your shops form an L, T, or + shape, each shop tile can only count toward **one** line — pick whichever grouping scores highest. Multiple separate lines each score independently.

**Common mistake:** Scattering shops across different rows and columns. Three isolated shops score only 6 points total, but three in a line score 10.

#### Factories (Gray) — Competitive Ranking
Factories are industrial buildings, and they score differently from everything else — it's a **competition across all cities in the game**. At the end, compare how many factories each city has:
- City with the **most** factories (or tied for most): **4 points per factory**
- **Second-most** (or tied for second): **3 points per factory**
- **All others**: **2 points per factory**

If all cities are tied, everyone scores 4 points per factory. A city with 0 factories scores nothing.

**Key insight:** Factory placement within your city grid doesn't matter — they don't need to be adjacent or clustered. What matters is how your **total count** compares to every other city at the table.

#### Parks (Green) — Connected Group Scoring
Parks are green spaces that score by forming **connected groups** — tiles that share a side (orthogonally adjacent, not diagonal). Unlike shops, parks don't need to form a straight line; any connected cluster works:
- 1 park: **2 points**
- 2 connected parks: **8 points**
- 3 connected parks: **12 points**
- 4 connected parks: **13 points**
- 5+ parks: **+1 point for each park beyond 3**

Multiple separate groups each score independently. Try to connect all your parks into one big group if you can — splitting them costs you points.

#### Taverns (Red) — Variety of Subtypes
Tavern tiles come in **4 subtypes** (music, drink, food, bed — each has a different icon). Taverns do NOT need to be adjacent to each other. Your score is based entirely on how many **different subtypes** you collect:
- 1 subtype: **1 point**
- 2 different subtypes: **4 points**
- 3 different subtypes: **9 points**
- All 4 subtypes (complete set): **17 points**

If you collect more than 4 taverns, extras start a new set. For example: 2 music + 1 food + 1 drink + 1 bed = one complete set (17 pts) + one leftover music (1 pt) = **18 points** total.

**Common mistake:** Grabbing multiple taverns of the same subtype thinking quantity helps. Three music taverns still only score 1 point. You need **different** subtypes.

#### Offices (Purple) — Cumulative Count + Tavern Bonus
Offices are professional buildings that score based on **how many total** you have in the city (they don't need to be adjacent):
- 1 office: **1 point**
- 2 offices: **3 points**
- 3 offices: **6 points**
- 4 offices: **10 points**
- 5 offices: **15 points**
- 6 offices: **21 points**

**Tavern adjacency bonus:** Here's where placement matters — each office that's adjacent to at least **1 tavern** scores **+1 bonus point**. An office can only earn this bonus once (even if it touches multiple taverns), but a single tavern can grant the bonus to several adjacent offices.

(So placing an office right next to a cluster of taverns is a smart move — the office gets its bonus, and those taverns are earning variety points on their own.)

#### Houses (Brown/Yellow) — Diversity Multiplier
Each house scores points equal to the number of **other unique tile types** present in the city — not counting houses themselves. If your city has all 5 other types (shops, factories, parks, taverns, offices), each house is worth **5 points**. If you only have 3 other types, each house is worth **3 points**.

**Factory penalty:** If a house is **adjacent to a factory**, that house drops to only **1 point total** regardless of diversity. (People don't enjoy living next to industrial buildings.) Keep your houses and factories separated!

Houses reward balanced city-building. Even a single tile of a missing type — one lonely office, one park — adds 1 point to **every** house in the city.

subtopics.title: Scoring & Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### When the Game Ends
The game always lasts exactly **3 rounds** — there's no variable end trigger. After Round 3, every city grid is fully built, and you move straight to scoring.

#### Scoring Each City
Grab your reference cards and score each city one at a time. Go through all 6 tile types:
1. **Shops** — Find every straight line of shops (same row or column). Apply the line-length scoring chart.
2. **Factories** — Compare factory counts across **all** cities at the table. Apply the rank-based per-factory scoring.
3. **Parks** — Identify each connected group of parks. Apply the group-size scoring chart.
4. **Taverns** — Count how many different subtypes you have. Apply the variety scoring chart. (Don't forget to score extra sets separately.)
5. **Offices** — Count total offices for cumulative scoring, then check each office for tavern adjacency bonuses (+1 each).
6. **Houses** — Count how many other unique tile types exist in the city. Multiply by number of houses. (But check for factory adjacency — those houses drop to 1 point each.)

Add up all 6 categories. That's the city's total score.

#### Your Personal Score
Here's the crucial part: your personal score is the **lower** of your two city scores. This is the central tension of the whole game — you **must** balance effort between both cities. A 40-40 split (personal score: 40) beats a 55-25 split (personal score: 25).

#### Winning
The player with the **highest personal score** wins.

#### Tiebreakers
1. The tied player whose **higher-scoring city** has more points wins. (This rewards balance — if you're tied on your low city, the player who kept both cities closer together likely wins.)
2. If still tied, **more combined total points** across both cities.
3. If still tied, **shared victory**.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Golden Rule: Balance Your Cities
Your score equals the **lower** of your two cities. This changes everything about how you make decisions:
- A 40-40 split (personal score: 40) beats a 55-25 split (personal score: 25).
- Every time you pick tiles, ask yourself: **which city needs more help right now?**
- When choosing your 2 tiles each pick, think about what **each** city needs. Picking 2 tiles that only benefit one city means you're neglecting the other.

#### Tile Priority Guide

**Houses are your safest bet.** As long as you maintain diversity (at least 1 of every other tile type), each house scores 5 points. Three houses in a diverse city = 15 easy points. Even if you're unsure what else to draft, a house is rarely a bad pick.

**Taverns need commitment.** A single tavern is a pitiful 1 point. Two different subtypes jump to 4 points. But a complete set of all 4 subtypes — music, drink, food, bed — scores an explosive **17 points**. Either commit to collecting variety or skip taverns entirely.

**Shops want straight lines.** Place your shops in the same row or column and keep extending that line. Three shops in a line = 10 points, but three scattered shops = only 6 points. Decide your line direction early and stick with it.

**Parks want connected clusters.** Unlike shops, parks can connect in any shape — L-shapes, blobs, whatever, as long as tiles share a side. A connected group of 3 parks = 12 points vs. 3 isolated parks = only 6.

**Factories are a gamble.** Having the most factories across all cities at the table gives you 4 points per factory — potentially huge. But if another city edges you out on count, your factories drop to 2-3 points each. Only invest heavily if you can win (or tie for) the top rank.

**Offices + Taverns synergize.** Offices scale well on their own (up to 21 points for 6), and each office adjacent to a tavern earns +1 bonus. Place taverns and offices near each other for reliable, high scoring.

#### Drafting Tips
- **Watch what you pass.** The tiles you don't pick go to your neighbor — who shares a city with you. Passing strong tiles to your left neighbor can directly help your shared left city in Round 1.
- **Hate-draft sparingly.** Denying tiles to distant opponents rarely matters as much as building your own cities well.
- **In Round 1, establish a plan.** Your first 2-3 tile placements set the direction for each city. Decide early: is this city going heavy taverns? Heavy houses? A balanced mix?

#### Common Mistakes
- **Pouring all your best tiles into one city.** Your neglected city drags your personal score down — remember, you're scored on the lower one.
- **Scattering shops and parks.** Isolated tiles score terribly. Shops need straight lines; parks need connected groups.
- **Ignoring house diversity.** 4 houses with only 2 other tile types = 4 x 2 = 8 points. Those same 4 houses with 5 other types = 4 x 5 = 20 points. Even a single office or factory tile can add 4 points to your house total.
- **Forgetting tavern subtypes.** 3 taverns of the **same** subtype still score only 1 point total. You need **different** subtypes for the big points.
- **Placing houses next to factories.** A house adjacent to a factory scores only 1 point instead of potentially 5. Keep them apart!
- **Not communicating with partners.** Talk about what the city needs, what's coming in the draft, and where tiles should go. Silence leads to bad cities.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Between Two Cities

**Getting Oriented**: You're sitting in a circle. Look to your left — you share a city with that player. Look to your right — you share a different city with that player. Your goal is to make **both** cities score well, because your personal score is the lower of the two. Grab your reference card and keep it face-up in front of you — it shows all 6 tile scoring rules, and you'll check it constantly.

**Round 1, First Pick**: Draw 7 single tiles from the supply. Fan them out and look at what you've got — identify the types (colors) and think about which tiles would help each city. Now pick 2 tiles, one destined for your left city and one for your right. Everyone reveals at the same time. Talk to each partner about where to place the tile in the 4x4 grid.

**A good first-pick approach**: Place a house in each city if you can. Houses are versatile — they'll score well as long as you diversify the city later. If you spot 2 taverns of different subtypes, grab both — that's 4 points of variety scoring instead of a lonely 1 point for a single tavern.

**Round 1 Continues**: Pass your remaining 5 tiles to the left. Pick 2 from the new hand you receive, reveal, place one in each city, pass left again. You'll do this 3 times total. On your last pick, you'll have 3 tiles — choose 2 and discard the last one face-down. Each city now has **5 single tiles**.

**Round 2 — Duplex Tiles**: Draw 3 duplex tiles. Each one shows 2 building types and covers 2 squares. Keep 2, discard 1. Place 1 duplex in your left city and 1 in your right. Think about what types each city is missing — a Shop/House duplex can extend a shop line **and** add diversity for your houses at the same time.

**Round 3 — Final Push**: Draw 7 new single tiles. Same drafting process as Round 1, but this time you pass to the **right**. This is your last chance to fill scoring gaps. Need that 4th tavern subtype for a 17-point complete set? Desperately need a park to bridge two disconnected groups? Prioritize whatever closes the biggest scoring gap in your weaker city.

**Scoring**: Grab your reference card and score each city type by type. Add up all 6 categories for each city's total. Your personal score is the **lower** city score. For your first game: 40-50 points is solid. 55+ is excellent.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Partner Paradox

Your score equals your **lower**-scoring city. This means a 60-point masterpiece paired with a 30-point disaster gives you a personal score of 30. Always invest in whichever city is currently weaker — even if the stronger city has a tempting scoring opportunity.

#### Tile Valuation by Game State

**Factories** score by competitive ranking across all cities — what matters is your **count** relative to everyone else, not where you place them in the grid. Three factories in a city that leads the table = 12 points (4 each). Those same three factories in a city that's third-place = only 6 points (2 each). Watch the table: if no one else is collecting factories, even 2-3 can win the top rank cheaply.

**Shops** want **straight lines** in the same row or column. A line of 3 = 10 points; a line of 4 = 15 points. Plan your line direction in Round 1 and protect that row or column for future shop placement. Scattered shops across different rows are wasted potential.

**Taverns** are pure set collection — 1/4/9/17 points for 1/2/3/4 unique subtypes. The jump from 3 types (9 pts) to a complete set (17 pts) is massive. If you're at 3 subtypes by Round 3, that missing 4th type is worth hunting hard for.

**Houses** score based on how many **other** building types exist in the city. They're strongest in diverse cities and weakest in specialized ones. A single tile of a missing type (one lonely park, one office) adds 1 point to **every** house — that's a great return on investment.

#### Draft Reading

Pay attention to what your neighbors are drafting. If your left neighbor is collecting factories, feed factories into your shared city too — you both benefit from a high factory count. If your right neighbor is ignoring taverns in your shared city, don't pour tavern tiles into a city where your partner won't help you finish the set.

#### Duplex Strategy

Duplex tiles cover 2 squares and can't be repositioned. In Round 2 your grid is only partially filled (5 of 16 squares), so you have flexibility — use it. A well-placed duplex can simultaneously extend a shop line, connect two park groups, or add a missing type for your houses. Duplex tiles that combine two types your city needs (like Park/Tavern when you're missing both) are especially valuable.

#### Round 3 Awareness

By Round 3, each city has 9 of 16 squares filled and you can see what every city at the table is building. Use this information:
- **Factory count check**: Count factories in all visible cities. If your city is close to the lead, one more factory could jump you from 2 pts/factory to 4 pts/factory — that's a swing of 6-8 points.
- **Fill scoring gaps in your weaker city first.** Your stronger city can absorb a mediocre tile; your weaker city can't.
- **Watch the draft direction.** Round 3 passes right (opposite of Round 1). The tiles you pass now go to the player who shares your **right** city.

#### The Kingmaker Trap

In the final picks, consider the scoreboard. If you can't win, avoid tile placements that hand victory to one specific opponent over another. Balanced, self-interested play earns trust at the table — and in a partnership game, that matters for future rounds and future games.
